benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of ancient civilizations, including Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China, by identifying their geographic locations and key contributions.
item_specs: Items assess identification of ancient civilizations' locations, achievements, and contributions to modern society.
essential_questions: How did geography influence the development of ancient civilizations?
What contributions from ancient civilizations still impact us today?
learning_targets: I can identify the geographic locations of major ancient civilizations.
I can describe key contributions of ancient civilizations to modern society.
sample_assessment: Which ancient civilization developed cuneiform, one of the earliest forms of writing? A) Egypt B) Mesopotamia C) China D) India

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of ancient Greece and Rome, including their governments, cultures, and lasting impacts on Western civilization.
item_specs: Items require comparing Greek and Roman governments and identifying their influences on modern democracy.
essential_questions: How did ancient Greek and Roman ideas shape modern government?
learning_targets: I can compare the governments of ancient Greece and Rome.
I can explain how Greek and Roman ideas influence modern Western civilization.
sample_assessment: Which type of government did ancient Athens practice? A) Monarchy B) Direct democracy C) Dictatorship D) Feudalism

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the major world religions, including their origins, beliefs, and geographic spread.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of the origins, basic beliefs, and geographic spread of major religions.
essential_questions: How did major world religions develop and spread?
learning_targets: I can identify the origins and basic beliefs of major world religions.
I can describe how religions spread through trade and conquest.
sample_assessment: Which three religions are considered monotheistic? A) Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism B) Judaism, Christianity, Islam C) Buddhism, Islam, Hinduism D) Confucianism, Judaism, Hinduism

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the medieval period in Europe, including feudalism, the role of the Church, and the Crusades.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of the feudal system, the role of the Catholic Church, and the impact of the Crusades.
essential_questions: How did feudalism organize medieval European society?
learning_targets: I can describe the feudal system and the roles within it.
I can explain the role of the Catholic Church in medieval Europe.
sample_assessment: In the feudal system, who worked the land in exchange for protection? A) Kings B) Knights C) Serfs D) Merchants

benchmark: The student will use maps, globes, and geographic tools to analyze the physical and cultural characteristics of world regions.
item_specs: Items require reading and interpreting maps, identifying geographic features, and analyzing spatial relationships.
essential_questions: How do geographic tools help us understand the world?
learning_targets: I can use maps and globes to locate countries, regions, and geographic features.
I can analyze how physical geography affects human settlement.
sample_assessment: Lines of latitude measure distance — A) East and west of the Prime Meridian B) North and south of the Equator C) Around the North Pole D) Along the International Date Line

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Renaissance, Reformation, and the Age of Exploration, including their causes and effects.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of causes and effects of the Renaissance, Reformation, and European exploration.
essential_questions: How did the Renaissance and Reformation change European society?
learning_targets: I can describe the causes and effects of the Renaissance.
I can explain how the Reformation changed religion and politics in Europe.
sample_assessment: Which invention helped spread Renaissance ideas throughout Europe? A) The compass B) The printing press C) The telescope D) Gunpowder

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Age of Revolution, including the Enlightenment, the American Revolution, and the French Revolution.
item_specs: Items require analyzing the influence of Enlightenment ideas on revolutionary movements.
essential_questions: How did Enlightenment ideas inspire revolutions?
learning_targets: I can explain how Enlightenment ideas influenced the American and French Revolutions.
I can compare the causes and outcomes of the American and French Revolutions.
sample_assessment: Which Enlightenment thinker argued that people have natural rights to life, liberty, and property? A) Voltaire B) Montesquieu C) John Locke D) Rousseau

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Industrial Revolution, including its causes, effects, and impact on urbanization and labor.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of the causes and social/economic effects of industrialization.
essential_questions: How did the Industrial Revolution transform society?
learning_targets: I can describe the causes of the Industrial Revolution.
I can explain the effects of industrialization on workers and cities.
sample_assessment: Which was a major effect of the Industrial Revolution? A) Decreased urbanization B) Growth of the factory system C) Return to feudalism D) Decline of trade

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of imperialism and its effects on colonized regions in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
item_specs: Items require analyzing the causes and effects of European imperialism on colonized peoples.
essential_questions: What motivated European imperialism, and what were its effects?
learning_targets: I can explain the motives behind European imperialism.
I can describe the effects of imperialism on colonized regions.
sample_assessment: The Berlin Conference of 1884-85 primarily dealt with — A) Ending World War I B) Dividing Africa among European powers C) Establishing the United Nations D) Creating trade agreements with China

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the causes, major events, and effects of World War I and World War II.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of causes, key events, and consequences of both world wars.
essential_questions: What caused the world wars, and how did they change the global order?
learning_targets: I can identify the causes and key events of World War I and World War II.
I can describe the effects of both world wars on the global order.
sample_assessment: Which was a direct cause of World War I? A) The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand B) The bombing of Pearl Harbor C) The fall of the Berlin Wall D) The Russian Revolution

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Cold War, including the competition between the United States and the Soviet Union and its global effects.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Cold War tensions, proxy wars, and the eventual collapse of the Soviet Union.
essential_questions: How did the Cold War shape global politics and alliances?
learning_targets: I can explain the ideological differences between the US and Soviet Union.
I can describe key events of the Cold War.
sample_assessment: The 'Iron Curtain' referred to the division between — A) North and South Korea B) Western and Eastern Europe C) The US and Mexico D) China and Japan

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the first permanent English settlement in America at Jamestown, Virginia, including the interactions between English settlers and Virginia Indians.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Jamestown's founding, challenges, and interactions with Powhatan peoples.
essential_questions: What challenges did early Virginia settlers face, and how did they interact with native peoples?
learning_targets: I can describe the founding and early challenges of Jamestown.
I can explain the interactions between English settlers and Virginia Indians.
sample_assessment: What cash crop helped Jamestown survive economically? A) Cotton B) Tobacco C) Rice D) Corn

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the American Revolution, including the key events, figures, and documents that led to American independence.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of causes, events, and outcomes of the American Revolution with emphasis on Virginia's role.
essential_questions: What ideas and events led to American independence?
learning_targets: I can identify the causes of the American Revolution.
I can explain the significance of key documents including the Declaration of Independence.
sample_assessment: The phrase 'No taxation without representation' expressed colonists' anger about — A) Being taxed by Parliament without having representatives there B) Paying too much for tea C) The quartering of soldiers D) Trade restrictions with France

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Constitution of the United States, including the principles of government, the Bill of Rights, and the amendment process.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of constitutional principles, the structure of government, and rights protected by the Bill of Rights.
essential_questions: How does the Constitution protect individual rights and organize government?
learning_targets: I can explain the principles of the Constitution including separation of powers and checks and balances.
I can identify rights protected by the Bill of Rights.
sample_assessment: Which amendment protects freedom of speech, religion, and the press? A) First Amendment B) Second Amendment C) Fourth Amendment D) Fifth Amendment

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the causes, course, and consequences of the Civil War, including Virginia's role in the conflict.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of the causes of the Civil War, major battles in Virginia, and the war's consequences.
essential_questions: What caused the Civil War, and how did it change the nation?
learning_targets: I can explain the causes of the Civil War including the role of slavery.
I can describe Virginia's role in the Civil War and key battles fought in the state.
sample_assessment: Where did General Robert E. Lee surrender to General Ulysses S. Grant, ending the Civil War? A) Gettysburg, PA B) Richmond, VA C) Appomattox Court House, VA D) Washington, DC

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of Reconstruction and its impact on the political, economic, and social life of the nation and Virginia.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Reconstruction policies, constitutional amendments, and their effects on Virginia.
essential_questions: How did Reconstruction attempt to rebuild the nation after the Civil War?
learning_targets: I can describe the goals and outcomes of Reconstruction.
I can explain how the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments changed American society.
sample_assessment: The 13th Amendment to the Constitution — A) Gave women the right to vote B) Abolished slavery C) Established Prohibition D) Granted citizenship to all born in the US

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the key political, economic, and social developments in Virginia from Reconstruction through the 20th century.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Virginia's development including industrialization, desegregation, and modern governance.
essential_questions: How has Virginia changed from Reconstruction to the present?
learning_targets: I can describe major political and social changes in Virginia since Reconstruction.
I can explain the impact of desegregation on Virginia.
sample_assessment: Virginia's policy of 'Massive Resistance' was a response to — A) The Industrial Revolution B) The Supreme Court's Brown v. Board of Education decision C) World War II D) The Great Depression

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Renaissance and its impact on Western civilization, including the revival of classical learning and the emergence of humanism.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Renaissance cultural achievements and the shift from medieval to modern thinking.
essential_questions: How did the Renaissance represent a break from medieval thinking?
learning_targets: I can describe the key features of the Renaissance.
I can explain how humanism changed European thought and culture.
sample_assessment: Renaissance humanism emphasized — A) Religious obedience above all else B) The potential and achievements of individuals C) Feudal loyalty D) Rejection of classical learning

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the political and philosophical ideas of the Enlightenment and their influence on the development of democratic governments.
item_specs: Items require connecting Enlightenment ideas to modern democratic principles and documents.
essential_questions: How did Enlightenment ideas shape modern government?
learning_targets: I can identify key Enlightenment thinkers and their ideas.
I can explain how Enlightenment ideas influenced democratic governments.
sample_assessment: Montesquieu's idea of separation of powers directly influenced — A) The feudal system B) The structure of the US government C) The Roman Republic D) The British monarchy

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the formation of the American constitutional government, including the debates between Federalists and Anti-Federalists.
item_specs: Items require analyzing the debates and compromises that shaped the Constitution.
essential_questions: What compromises shaped the Constitution?
learning_targets: I can explain the key debates at the Constitutional Convention.
I can compare Federalist and Anti-Federalist positions.
sample_assessment: The Great Compromise at the Constitutional Convention resolved a dispute over — A) Slavery B) Representation in Congress C) Presidential power D) Judicial review

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of westward expansion, including its impact on Native Americans, the concept of Manifest Destiny, and territorial acquisitions.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of territorial growth, its justifications, and its impact on native peoples.
essential_questions: How did Manifest Destiny drive westward expansion, and at what cost?
learning_targets: I can explain the concept of Manifest Destiny.
I can describe the impact of westward expansion on Native American peoples.
sample_assessment: The Trail of Tears refers to — A) The journey of settlers to California B) The forced relocation of Cherokee and other Native American nations C) The route of the transcontinental railroad D) The path of Lewis and Clark

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the major events and turning points of World War I and World War II, including the impact on the home front and Virginia.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of US involvement in both world wars and their impact on American society and Virginia.
essential_questions: How did the world wars change America and Virginia?
learning_targets: I can describe the causes and effects of US involvement in WWI and WWII.
I can explain the impact of the wars on the home front and Virginia.
sample_assessment: What event directly led to US entry into World War II? A) The sinking of the Lusitania B) The attack on Pearl Harbor C) The invasion of Poland D) The Treaty of Versailles

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the Civil Rights movement, including key leaders, events, and legislation that advanced equality.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of the Civil Rights movement, its leaders, strategies, and legislative achievements.
essential_questions: How did the Civil Rights movement advance equality in America?
learning_targets: I can identify key leaders and events of the Civil Rights movement.
I can explain the significance of Civil Rights legislation.
sample_assessment: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 primarily outlawed — A) Child labor B) Discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin C) Alcohol sales D) Immigration from Asia

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the foundations of American constitutional government, including the principles of democracy and the rule of law.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of foundational principles including popular sovereignty, limited government, and rule of law.
essential_questions: What principles form the foundation of American government?
learning_targets: I can explain the principles of American democracy.
I can describe how the Constitution limits government power.
sample_assessment: The principle that government power comes from the people is called — A) Federalism B) Popular sovereignty C) Judicial review D) Separation of powers

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the structure and powers of the national government, including the legislative, executive, and judicial branches.
item_specs: Items require identifying the powers of each branch and explaining how checks and balances work.
essential_questions: How do the three branches of government share and check power?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure and powers of each branch of government.
I can explain how checks and balances prevent abuse of power.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of checks and balances? A) The President signing a bill into law B) The Supreme Court declaring a law unconstitutional C) Congress passing a bill D) A governor giving a speech

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of state and local government in Virginia, including the structure of Virginia's government and the Virginia Constitution.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of Virginia's governmental structure, including the General Assembly, governor, and court system.
essential_questions: How is Virginia's government structured, and how does it serve its citizens?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure of Virginia's state government.
I can explain the relationship between state and local government in Virginia.
sample_assessment: Virginia's legislature is called the — A) State Senate B) General Assembly C) House of Representatives D) Parliament

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the rights and responsibilities of citizens in a democratic society, including voting, civic participation, and the judicial process.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of citizen rights, responsibilities, and the importance of civic engagement.
essential_questions: What are the rights and responsibilities of citizens in a democracy?
learning_targets: I can explain the rights and responsibilities of US citizens.
I can describe the importance of civic participation including voting.
sample_assessment: Which is a responsibility, not just a right, of US citizens? A) Freedom of speech B) Freedom of religion C) Serving on a jury when called D) Owning property

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of fundamental economic concepts, including scarcity, opportunity cost, supply and demand, and the role of incentives.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of basic economic concepts and their application to everyday decisions.
essential_questions: How do scarcity and incentives affect economic decisions?
learning_targets: I can explain how scarcity forces individuals and societies to make choices.
I can describe how supply and demand determine prices.
sample_assessment: When the demand for a product increases while supply stays the same, the price will — A) Decrease B) Increase C) Stay the same D) Drop to zero

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the different types of economic systems, including market, command, and mixed economies.
item_specs: Items require comparing economic systems and identifying characteristics of each type.
essential_questions: How do different economic systems answer the basic economic questions?
learning_targets: I can compare market, command, and mixed economic systems.
I can identify the role of government in each type of economic system.
sample_assessment: In a market economy, prices are primarily determined by — A) The government B) Supply and demand C) Tradition D) International organizations

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of the role of government in the economy, including taxation, regulation, and fiscal and monetary policy.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of how government policies affect the economy, including the roles of the Federal Reserve and Congress.
essential_questions: How does the government influence economic activity?
learning_targets: I can explain how fiscal and monetary policy affect the economy.
I can describe the role of the Federal Reserve.
sample_assessment: The Federal Reserve can slow down inflation by — A) Lowering interest rates B) Raising interest rates C) Printing more money D) Reducing taxes

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of personal finance concepts, including budgeting, saving, investing, credit, and the importance of financial literacy.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of personal financial decision-making and financial planning.
essential_questions: How do financial decisions impact individuals and families?
learning_targets: I can create and explain a personal budget.
I can describe the benefits and risks of saving, investing, and using credit.
sample_assessment: Which action BEST demonstrates responsible use of credit? A) Paying only the minimum payment each month B) Using a credit card for all purchases C) Paying the full balance each month D) Opening multiple credit cards at once

benchmark: The student will demonstrate knowledge of international trade, including the concepts of comparative advantage, tariffs, and the effects of globalization.
item_specs: Items assess understanding of why nations trade, the effects of trade barriers, and the impact of globalization.
essential_questions: Why do nations trade, and what are the effects of globalization?
learning_targets: I can explain the concept of comparative advantage.
I can analyze the effects of tariffs and trade agreements on economies.
sample_assessment: A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good when it can — A) Produce more of it than any other country B) Produce it at a lower opportunity cost than other countries C) Sell it at the highest price D) Import it cheaply from other nations
